coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots from lotus root for Hg(II) ions detection and cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Gu, Dan
Shang, Shaoming
Yu, Qin
Shen, Jie
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a facile, green, and fast method was developed in the synthesis of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDs) with nitrogen content of 5.23%, using one-pot microwave treatment of lotus root (LR), without using any other surface passivation agents. The results show that these LR-CDs (with an average diameter of 9.41nm) possess many outstanding features and have a high quantum yield of 19.0%. We further demonstrated applications of LR-CDs as probes for heavy metal ion detection. The LR-CDs exhibit captivating sensitivity and selectivity toward Hg2+ with a linear range from 0.1 to 60.0μM and a detection limit of 18.7nM. Eventually, the LR-CDs were applied for multicolor cell imaging, demonstrating their potential toward diverse applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Lotus root
Hg2+
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of the LR-CDs to the Hg2+ and fluorescence quenching mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of LR-CDs and cells imaging and Hg2+ detection.

(a) Typical TEM image (the inset was particle size distribution histogram of the LR-CDs). (b) The FTIR spectra of the LR-CDs. (c) UV/Vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of LR-CDs in aqueous solution. Inset photograph exhibits the LR-CDs under visible light (left) and UV lamp (right) at 360nm wavelength. (d) Emission spectra of the LR-CDs at different excitation wavelength from 280 to 440nm.

(a) Fluorescence responses of LR-CDs to the addition of different metal ions under PBS (pH 7.0, 10mmolL−1). The concentration of each metal ion is 50μmolL−1. F0 and F correspond to the fluorescence intensities of LR-CDs at λem of 435nm excited at 360nm in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively; (b) Emission spectra of LR-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of Hg2+ (from 0 to 60.0μmolL−1) in aqueous solution. (Inset: plots of intensity ratio F/F0 versus the concentrations of Hg2+). The relationship between the F/F0 and Hg2+ concentrations (c: 0.1–1.5μM; d: 2.0–60.0μM).
Bright field and fluorescence microscope images of A549 cells incubated with LR-CDs for 24h.
Recovery tests of spiked Hg2+ in tap water measured with LR-CDs and HG-AFS, respectively (n=3).

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots from lotus root for Hg(II) ions detection and cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Laboratory of Food Colloids and Biotechnology, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Laboratory of Food Colloids and Biotechnology
School of Chemical and Material Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots were synthesized by a facile, green, and fast microwave method, using lotus root as carbon source. These nitrogen-doped carbon dots can be used for Hg2+ detection and cell imaging.

Herein, a facile, green, and fast method was developed in the synthesis of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDs) with nitrogen content of 5.23%, using one-pot microwave treatment of lotus root (LR), without using any other surface passivation agents. The results show that these LR-CDs (with an average diameter of 9.41nm) possess many outstanding features and have a high quantum yield of 19.0%. We further demonstrated applications of LR-CDs as probes for heavy metal ion detection. The LR-CDs exhibit captivating sensitivity and selectivity toward Hg2+ with a linear range from 0.1 to 60.0μM and a detection limit of 18.7nM. Eventually, the LR-CDs were applied for multicolor cell imaging, demonstrating their potential toward diverse applications.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new kind of nanomaterial in the nanocarbon family, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have attracted a lot of attention due to their intriguing properties such as low toxicity, robust chemical stability, outstanding environment friendly, high resistance to photo-bleaching, and excellent biocompatibility compared with heavy metal based quantum dots and organic dyes [1–3]. In this context, the wide potential of CDs has been exploited in the fields of bio-imaging [4], drug delivery [5], optoelectronic and energy devices [6], fluorescent ink [7], catalysis [8], and sensor [9–12]. Currently, a broad series of approaches for obtaining fluorescent CDs have been developed. For instance, arc discharge [13], laser ablation [14], and plasma treatment [15], electrochemical oxidation [16], hydrothermal [17] or acidic oxidation [18], and microwave irradiation [7]. Among them, microwave irradiation strategy is considered to be a simple, direct, and efficient way to obtain carbon nanoparticles [19]. In addition, microwave irradiation could achieve the fabricated of nanomaterials in an extremely short time based on the intensive and efficient energy.

Recently, much effort has been put into developing green methodologies for preparing CDs from natural sources which are widely available and harmless to the environment [20,21]. However, most of these CDs have a relatively low quantum yield (QY), which limits their practical large-scale applications. Nitrogen-doped CDs have attracted enormous attention for its capacity to enhance fluorescent quantum yields (FLQY) of CDs [22] and some sensing applications via the versatile coordinating behavior of terminal amine groups [23]. The nitrogen-doped CDs have been commonly synthesized by surface passivation and functionalization of CDs using nitrogenous chemical reagents [4]. Although nitrogenous chemical reagents can enhance the quantum yield of CDs, it suffers from a lot of drawbacks, e.g., time-consumption, complicated procedures and potential hazard from the chemicals for the following biological applications [24]. Consequently, miscellaneous nitrogen-containing carbon-enriched green biomass materials have been employed as the precursors in the preparation of CDs for the improvement of QY [8,25].

In this article, we used economical lotus root as a carbon source for microwave synthesis of hydrosoluble high-fluorescent nitrogen-doped LR-CDs without using any surface passivation agents (Fig. 1 ). Lotus root is an inexpensive common food and easy to get. It contains many chemical components, e.g. amino acids, alkaloid, glucoprotein, and polysaccharides, which serve as abundant sources of carbon and nitrogen for the preparation of CDs. Furthermore, the prepared LR-CDs can serve as a very effective fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ with a linear range from 0.1 to 60.0μM and a detection limit of 18.7nM. In order to show the practicality of this fluorescence probe, it is further applied to measure the trace Hg2+ in tap water sample. Meanwhile, the LR-CDs were employed for cell imaging, exhibiting their potential in biomedical fields.

Including of materials and reagents; instrumentation; preparation of LR-CDs; quantum yield measurements; detection of Hg2+; detection of Hg2+ in real samples; cellular toxicity test; cellular imaging are shown in the Supporting information.

The transmission electron microscopy has been used to characterize the morphology and size of the LR-CDs. The TEM image shows that LR-CDs particles are well dispersed spherical particles with a size distribution within the range of 5–15nm and an average diameter of about 9.41nm (Fig. 2 a). In order to investigated the components and structures of the synthesized LR-CDs, Fourier transformed infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were then examined. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2b) exhibits characteristic absorption bands of OH and NH stretching vibrations at 3257cm−1, CH stretching vibrations at 2919cm−1, COO asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration at 1580cm−1 and 1400cm−1, respectively. The absorptions at 1299cm−1, 1105cm−1 and 1027cm−1 display COH bending vibration. The XPS spectrum provides more convincing evidence for the N-doping surface state of LR-CDs (Figs. S1a–S1c). The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. S1a) shows a predominant graphitic C1s peak at 284.9eV, an obvious N1s peak at 399.9eV, and an O1s peak at 531.9eV. The relative contents of C, N and O in the LR-CDs are 61.81%, 5.23% and 32.96%, respectively. The C1s XPS spectrum displays four different types of carbon atoms (Fig. S1b): CC/CC with a binding energy at 284.5eV; CO at 286.2eV; CO at 287.9eV; and CN at 285.2eV, demonstrating the presence of sp2 C and amide linkage. The N1s spectrum exhibits two peaks at 399.7eV and 401.0eV (Fig. S1c), which are ascribed to CNC and surface NH groups, respectively, implying that N atoms had been partly doped into the LR-CDs. In general, the FT-IR data showed satisfactory agreement with XPS results, intensively suggesting that there are characteristic functional groups including COOH, OH and NH2 on the surface of the prepared LR-CDs in this work. These functional groups improve the hydrophilicity and stability of the LR-CDs, which accounted for the great potential of the material for sensing in aqueous samples.

In the UV–vis absorption spectrum, the peak is located at 280nm, which is attributed to the contribution of surface groups and the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains. The fluorescence emission spectrum of LR-CDs exhibits strong peak at 435nm, and the corresponding excitation wavelength is 360nm (Fig. 2c). Excitation-wavelength-dependent photoluminescence behavior was observed (Fig. 2d), which is common for fluorescent carbon materials [26]. These behaviors resulted from the different sizes of nanodots and a distribution of different surface states due to the different organic groups on LR-CDs´ surface [13]. These fluorescence properties of the LR-CDs make them particularly useful for optical bio-imaging [27]. The fluorescence lifetime of LR-CDs is measured to be 3.87ns at 435nm under the 375nm excitation (Fig. S1d). The lifetime in the magnitude of nanosecond suggests that the as-prepared LR-CDs are suitable for optoelectronic and biological application [28]. Compared with the CDs synthesized from other natural materials, the LR-CDs have a high QY of 19.0%. The high quantum yield is possibly due to the existence of nitrogen-containing functional groups, which come from lotus root rather than other surface passivation reagents. We have made an investigation about the reported CDs prepared from natural materials, as listed in Table S1. The high quantum yield may be beneficial to cell imaging and improve the sensitivity for detection Hg2+.

We further researched the fluorescent properties of LR-CDs under different conditions. The effect of pH on the fluorescence behavior of the LR-CDs was also investigated (Fig. S2a). The PL intensity of LR-CDs increase when pH is risen from 1 to 6 and a further to some extent its remains the same as from 6 to 10. Afterwards, the pH caused a decrease in fluorescence intensity of LR-CDs. As revealed (Figs. S2b–S2c), the fluorescent intensities of LR-CDs scarcely exhibited variation along with varying concentrations of NaCl and continuous irradiation time by UV-lamp, demonstrating the LR-CDs captivating stability. These results reveal that the LR-CDs have a high ability for sensing applications under physiological conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of the LR-CDs to the Hg2+ and fluorescence quenching mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Interestingly, we found that Hg2+ can obviously quench the fluorescence of the LR-CDs, revealing the LR-CDs can be potentially used as an Hg2+ sensor. To evaluate the selectivity of the present sensing system, the fluorescence intensities of LR-CDs were inspected in the presence of a variety of metal ions including Hg2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Sr2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Fe2+, Co2+ and Zn2+ at the same concentrations (50μmolL−1), respectively. Results in Fig. 3 a exhibit that only Hg2+ induce noticeable quenching while other metal ions show no or little quenching effects. Fig. S2d displays the results of interference testes. Similarly, the fluorescence intensities of LR-CDs have no obviously change after mixed with any one of the interference metal ions. However the significant fluorescence quenching is observed on further addition of Hg2+ in each of the testing solutions. These results show outstanding selectivity of the present fluorescent probe toward the sensor for Hg2+. To determine Hg2+ effectively, other conditions including reaction time, pH and buffers were optimized. It costs only 150s to reach quenching equilibrium, indicating that the quenching rate is fairly fast (Fig. S3a). It could be observed that the optimum quenching efficiency was resulted in pH 7.0 (Fig. S3b). Finally, the effects of various buffers at pH 7.0 were tested, and Δ F of the LR-CDs only in PBS buffer reached maximum rather than that in other buffers (Fig. S3c). Hence, the 10mmolL−1 of PBS in pH 7.0 was chosen as the aqueous medium for Hg2+ detection.

In order to gain further perception in to the fluorescence quenching mechanism of LR-CDs by Hg2+, fluorescence lifetime measurements were carried out. As shown in Fig. S1d, the fluorescence lifetime of the blank LR-CDs is 3.87ns and 3.85ns in the present of Hg2+. These results indicate that the fluorescence quenching of LR-CDs by Hg2+ is a static quenching process. Therefore, high selectivity of LR-CDs is due to the more powerful affinity and faster chelating kinetics of Hg2+ than other metal ions toward the carboxylic, hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface of the LR-CDs [29]. Besides, the redox potentials of Hg2+/Hg+ (Ф=0.91) is located between the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) of LR-CDs, causing photo-induced electron transfer from CB to the complex states of Hg2+ [30]. As a result, the fluorescence is quenched. In order to further understand the binding between LR-CDs and Hg2+, the UV–vis absorption spectrums of LR-CDs and LR-CDs-Hg2+ were recorded. As displayed in Fig. S3d, the absorption peak of LR-CDs is located at 280nm. However, when Hg2+ was added in to the solution of LR-CDs, the absorption peak of LR-CDs disappeared remarkably, demonstrating the binding of LR-CDs with Hg2+.

For sensitivity study, different concentrations of Hg2+ within 60.0μM were examined. Fig. 3b shows the fluorescence intensity of the LR-CDs is sensitive to Hg2+ and decrease with the increasing concentrations of Hg2+ upon the excitation at 360nm. Fig. 3c and d illustrates the detection of Hg2+ with two linear ranges of 0.1–1.5μM (F/F0 =0.99624–0.12851×[Hg2+], R2 =0.9919) and 2.0–60.0μM (F/F0 =0.78335–0.00781×[Hg2+], R2 =0.994), respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) is estimated to be 18.7nM (at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3). As listed in Table S2, these results are comparable with other assays in the literature. Evidently, the developed assay is superior, with the advantages of low detection limit and wide linear range.

To verify the utilization of prepared LR-CDs sensor for environmental Hg2+, the practical application of this sensing system for Hg2+ detection was investigated using tap water from our lab (Table 1 ). As listed, the recoveries of Hg2+ in the spiked samples are in the range of 96.9–99.0%. To verify the accuracy and dependability of the proposed method we also use hydride generation-atomic fluorescence spectrometry (HG-AFS) to analyze the spiked tap water for comparison. Table 1 exhibits that the analytical result of this approach is in line with that of the HG-AFS method. These results show that this sensing system has significant potential for quantitative detection of Hg2+ in environmental samples.

In vitro cellular uptake of LR-CDs in A549 cells was observed by fluorescence microscopy. Fig. 4 shows the images of A549 cells after the incubation with LR-CDs. At the 330–385nm, 450–480nm and 510–550nm excitation, bright blue, green, and red luminescence in the cell membrane and cytoplasm area were observed, respectively. The results indicate that the CDs derived from lotus root can servers as an excellent fluorescence imaging probe. Moreover, an MTT (methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium) assay showed that the LR-CDs possess low cytotoxicity, and thus could act as an excellent bioimaging reagent (Fig. S4).

In summary, we have creatively synthesized nitrogen-doped LR-CDs based on lotus root via a simple, green and fast microwave method without using other surface passivation agents. Additionally, the LR-CDs show brilliant selectivity and sensitivity and were used as fluorescent probes to detect Hg2+ in aqueous solutions with the detection limit of 18.7nM. Explorations of Hg2+ detection in tape water illustrated the ability of this sensor to work effectively under the daily water environment. In addition, the LR-CDs were successfully applied for multicolor living cell imaging. These results demonstrate that the prepared LR-CDs may be used as an eco-friendly material for the potential in vitro or in vivo multicolor imaging and sensing applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2016.08.012.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
